meta.description: Act 2 Chapter 1 — Consolidation. Three months after the Transit Hub riot. Martial law, job promotions, the LATENCY informant, and faction missions.

start.title: Three Months Later
text: Three months since the riot. Three months since the Transit Hub burned and the registration centers closed their doors and the city held its breath. Three months, and the city has not exhaled.

Martial law came on the seventh day. Director Voss appeared on every screen with that same rehearsed calm, announced Meridian Emergency Protocol Nine, and turned the drones from watchers into wardens. Curfew at sundown. Checkpoints on every transit line. Patrols in the streets, the alleys, the tunnels. The sky above the Lower District hums day and night with the sound of surveillance rotors.

Your district is scarred. Blast marks on the walls of the Transit Hub that nobody has painted over. A memorial of burnt candles and scratched names on the platform where seventeen people died. The clinic overflows. The markets are half-shuttered. People talk in whispers now, even in their own homes.
But you survived. And survival in this city is a currency all its own.

text.text: Mako's shop is still open, though the front window is boarded up where a drone round punched through it. She works with a new tension in her shoulders, a tightness that wasn't there before. She saw things in the riot that she doesn't talk about. But she's alive. That counts.

Kira survived too. She's changed. The sheltered heiress who stumbled into the Lower District is gone. In her place is someone harder, someone who watched people die in the Hub and decided that running back to the Cloud Towers was no longer an option.

People remember what you did at the barricade. The story has grown in the telling, as stories do, but the core is true: you stood when others ran. In the Lower District, that kind of reputation is armor thicker than anything Hammer could forge.
The retreat you helped organize saved dozens of lives. People you've never met nod to you in the street. The ones who made it out of the Hub because of you haven't forgotten. They won't.

choices.text: Survey the district from the rooftop

act2_002.title: The Scarred City
text: From the rooftop you can see the new shape of the city. Meridian drones patrol in overlapping figure-eight patterns, their formation so precise it looks choreographed. Every thirty seconds a scanner beam sweeps the street below, logging faces, checking registrations, building a map of every living thing that moves.

The Transit Hub has been rebuilt. Not repaired, rebuilt. Meridian tore down the old structure and erected something sleeker, harder, with internal checkpoints and biometric gates. The new Hub is a fortress wearing the skin of a transit station.
To the north, the Cloud Towers gleam unchanged. The rich do not suffer martial law. Their drones are concierge models, not combat variants. Their checkpoints are velvet ropes, not razor wire.
To the south, the Undercity has gone darker. The tunnel entrances are sealed with Meridian blast doors. Whatever is happening below the streets is happening in silence.

text.text: Your faction has adapted. Three months is long enough to lick wounds, reorganize, and start planning. The messages on your encrypted comm channel have shifted from grief to strategy. Something is coming.

Without a faction banner, you've had to navigate the new order alone. It's harder. It's also freer. Every faction has approached you in the last three months. Your independence makes them nervous and curious in equal measure.

choices.text: Head to your quarters

act2_003.title: The Message
text: Your quarters are modest but secure. Three months of careful work have turned a dingy room into something defensible: reinforced door, signal jammer on the window, emergency pack under the bed. You've learned from the riot. Everyone has.

You drop onto the cot and check your datapad. The usual chatter: market prices, curfew schedules, rumor feeds. Then something different. A message in a format you don't recognize, routed through so many proxy nodes that the origin is untraceable. No sender ID. No subject line. Just text.
"Project LATENCY is real. They're not just storing memories. They're rewriting them."
You read it again. A third time. The words don't change.

"The stack transfer process is not preservation. It is alteration. Every consciousness that passes through a Meridian cortical stack is modified. Loyalty parameters inserted. Behavioral constraints encoded. The rich aren't just living forever. They're being slowly rewritten into loyal servants of the system that sells them immortality."
"If you want proof, find me. Stack Clinic. Night shift. Come alone."
The message deletes itself. Your datapad's memory shows no trace it ever existed.

choices.text: Think about what this means

act2_004.title: Processing the Revelation
text: You sit in the dark and think. If the message is true, it changes everything. The cortical stack, the foundation of immortality for the wealthy, isn't preservation. It's a leash. Every stack transfer, every time a rich citizen moves their consciousness to a new body, Meridian rewrites them. A little more loyal. A little more compliant. A little less human.

It would explain the uniformity of the upper class. The way stack-lords who were once fierce competitors become eerily cooperative after enough transfers. The way the oldest families in the Cloud Towers all seem to share the same quiet, unsettling calm.
It would also explain why Meridian fought so hard to make registration mandatory. Mapping every brain in the city isn't just surveillance. It's preparation. The first step toward rewriting everyone.

But the message could also be a trap. Meridian's intelligence division has been hunting dissidents since the riot. A message like this, designed to draw out anyone interested in fighting back, would be exactly their style.
Either way, the Stack Clinic at night. That's the next move. But first, you have work to do.

choices.text: Head to work. The day waits for no one under martial law.

act2_005.title: The New Order
text: The street is grey with morning smog and drone exhaust. Checkpoint Delta-Seven stands between you and the transit line: two Meridian guards in full tactical gear flanking a biometric scanner. The scanner reads your face, your gait, your heartbeat. It knows you before you reach it.
"Clear," the scanner announces. The guards don't even look at you. You're in the system. You're compliant. You pass.

Three months of martial law have made this routine. The anger hasn't faded, but it has gone underground, compressed into something denser and more dangerous. People comply on the surface. Underneath, the city simmers.

Your work awaits. Three months have changed more than the city. They've changed your place in it. The riot made reputations and broke them. Yours was made. And the people who noticed are offering you a step up.

choices.text: Continue to work
Continue to work
Continue to work
Continue to work
Continue to work

act2_006.title: Enforcer: Squad Leader
text: The barracks are busier than they've ever been. Martial law means more patrols, more checkpoints, more bodies needed on the street. The district security chief, a scarred woman named Captain Drask, waves you into her office before you even reach your locker.

"You performed well during the riot," she says, not looking up from her terminal. "Kept your head when half my people lost theirs. I'm promoting you to Squad Leader. Rank One. You'll command a five-person patrol unit in the Lower District."

She finally looks at you. Her eyes are tired and hard. "There's a catch. Meridian wants us cracking down on unregistered civilians. Rounding up people who missed the compliance deadline. Families, workers, kids. My orders say 'detain and process.' Your conscience says whatever it says. But the drones are watching you too, Squad Leader. Remember that."
She slides a tactical datapad across the desk. Your first patrol route. Your first squad. Your first impossible choice.

choices.text: [Charisma 15] Convince Drask to assign your squad to infrastructure patrol instead of civilian roundups
Accept the assignment without comment. Play along for now.
Tell Drask you'll handle it your way. She doesn't need the details.

act2_007a.title: Enforcer: Compassionate Authority
text: Drask studies you for a long moment. Something shifts behind her eyes, something that might be relief.

"Infrastructure patrol. Fine. But you'll still encounter non-compliant civilians. What you do with them is your call, as long as the reports look clean." She taps her terminal. "I'm assigning you Sector Nine. The residential blocks near the old market. Lots of families. Lots of people who couldn't register because the centers were shut down during the riot and never reopened in their area."
She leans forward. "I'm giving you this because I think you'll do the right thing. Don't make me regret it."

Your first patrol goes smoothly. You find unregistered families and help them navigate the system instead of cuffing them. Word spreads in the district: the new Squad Leader isn't a Meridian dog. That reputation is a shield and a target in equal measure.

choices.text: Finish your shift and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_007b.title: Enforcer: The Roundup
text: Your first patrol takes you through the densest residential blocks in the Lower District. Your squad is a mix of veterans and recruits, all of them watching you to see what kind of leader you are.

You find them in the third building. A family of five, huddled in a two-room apartment. The father has a prosthetic leg that doesn't work right. The mother holds a baby. Two children stare at you from behind a threadbare curtain.
"We tried to register," the father says, his voice hollow. "The center in our sector never reopened after the riot. We've been trying."
Your squad looks at you. The drones overhead log every second. The tactical datapad says: detain and process. The father's eyes say something else entirely.

You process the family as 'pending registration — civilian cooperation noted.' It's not a pardon. It's a delay. It buys them a week. Maybe two. But the paperwork will satisfy Meridian's algorithms, and the family stays together tonight.
Your squad says nothing. But on the walk back, one of the veterans gives you a nod that means more than words.

choices.text: Finish your shift and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_007c.title: Enforcer: Playing Both Sides
text: You run your squad with deliberate ambiguity. On paper, your reports are flawless: patrols conducted, checkpoints maintained, compliance metrics trending upward. In practice, you warn people before your squad arrives. You lose paperwork. You develop convenient communication failures with Meridian central during the most sensitive operations.

It's a dangerous game. Every falsified report is a thread that could unravel. Every warned civilian is a potential leak. But three months of survival have taught you something the Academy never did: the system has gaps because the system is run by people, and people can be predicted.
Your squad figures it out within the first week. They don't say anything. Two of them start helping. The other three keep their heads down. Nobody reports you. Not yet.

choices.text: Finish your shift and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_008.title: Hacker: Deeper Networks
text: The underground server room is three levels below a condemned laundromat. Your new workspace. After the riot, the network you operated on was compromised. Meridian traced half the nodes and burned them. But the core survived, and the people who ran it remembered who kept the lines open during the worst of it.
Your handler, a faceless voice called Switchboard, appears as text on your terminal: RANK 1 ACCESS GRANTED. WELCOME TO THE DEEP LAYER.

The deep layer is where the real traffic moves. Corporate espionage. Faction intelligence. Meridian's own internal communications, skimmed from poorly secured relay stations. It's a level of access that would get you killed ten times over if anyone traced it back to you.

Switchboard's next message freezes you: FOUND SOMETHING IN MERIDIAN SUBNET. AI DESIGNATION: PANOPTICON. ACTIVE SURVEILLANCE INTELLIGENCE. IT'S NOT JUST WATCHING THE CITY. IT'S PREDICTING IT. MODELING BEHAVIOR. ASSIGNING THREAT SCORES TO EVERY CITIZEN IN REAL TIME.
A file attaches itself to the message. PANOPTICON's architecture. Partial, corrupted, but enough to see the shape of it. Meridian hasn't just deployed drones. They've deployed a god.

choices.text: [Tech 15] Attempt to probe PANOPTICON's outer defenses without triggering an alert
Log the data and back out carefully. Study it before engaging.

act2_009a.title: Hacker: Inside the Machine
text: Your probe slips through PANOPTICON's outer layer like a needle through silk. The AI's defenses are formidable but designed for brute-force intrusion attempts. Your approach is surgical, a passive listener that mimics legitimate system traffic.

What you see inside makes your blood run cold. PANOPTICON doesn't just monitor. It models every citizen as a behavioral node in a predictive network. It knows where you'll be tomorrow based on where you were yesterday. It knows who you'll talk to based on who you've talked to before. It assigns threat probability scores updated every eleven seconds.
Your threat score: 67.4. Elevated but not critical. The system flags you as 'potential dissident — monitor' but hasn't escalated to 'neutralize.' Yet.
You pull out before the next security sweep. Your hands are shaking. You've just looked into the eye of something vast and cold and patient, and it almost looked back.

choices.text: Log off and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_009b.title: Hacker: Burned
text: Your probe touches something it shouldn't. A trip wire you didn't see, buried in a subnet that looked dormant. The response is immediate: your screen floods with counter-intrusion alerts. PANOPTICON saw you.
You kill the connection. Wipe the terminal. Burn the node. Standard emergency protocol. By the time Meridian's trace-back reaches this relay, you'll be three nodes away on a different subnet.

But the damage is done. PANOPTICON logged your intrusion signature. It doesn't know who you are, not yet, but it knows someone was looking. And an AI that predicts behavior will be watching for the next attempt.
Switchboard's message arrives thirty seconds later: YOUR THREAT SCORE JUST JUMPED. LAY LOW. DO NOT ACCESS THE DEEP LAYER FOR 48 HOURS.
You sit in the dark of the server room and listen to the ventilation system hum. Somewhere in Meridian's servers, an algorithm is thinking about you. That's a new kind of fear.

choices.text: Leave the server room and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_009c.title: Hacker: Patient Analysis
text: You resist the urge to poke the sleeping god. Instead, you download every byte of the partial architecture file and begin analyzing it offline, on an air-gapped terminal with no network connection. Old school. Unhackable.

Hours pass. The picture that forms is disturbing. PANOPTICON is not a traditional AI. It's a distributed intelligence running across Meridian's entire infrastructure: drones, checkpoints, building sensors, even the public terminals. Every device in the city is a neuron in its brain. It doesn't have a central server to attack. It IS the city's digital nervous system.
The implications are staggering. To fight PANOPTICON, you'd have to fight the infrastructure itself. Or find a way to make it work for you.
You save your analysis to an encrypted chip and pocket it. This information is worth more than credits. It's worth lives.

choices.text: Head out and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_010.title: Medic: The Clinic
text: The clinic hasn't closed since the riot. Three months of continuous operation, running on donated supplies, volunteer labor, and the kind of stubborn refusal to die that defines the Lower District. You've been here for most of it.

Today, the old clinic director, Dr. Vasquez, calls you into his office. He looks like he hasn't slept since martial law began. "I'm stepping back," he says. "My hands shake. My judgment slips. You've been running this place in everything but name for weeks. Time to make it official."

He slides a keycard across the desk. Clinic director. Rank One. Full authority over medical decisions, supply allocation, and patient triage. Also full responsibility for keeping a hundred people alive every day with supplies meant for twenty.

"One more thing," Vasquez says. "There's a sickness spreading in the lower blocks. Respiratory, progressive, doesn't respond to standard treatment. Started three weeks ago. Fifteen cases. Now it's forty. I don't know what it is." He pauses. "But I know what it looks like when a disease hits only poor people in a specific geographic area. It looks engineered."

choices.text: [Wisdom 14] Analyze the disease pattern to determine if it's engineered
Focus on treatment first. Saving lives comes before finding answers.

act2_011a.title: Medic: Engineered Plague
text: You spend the night cross-referencing patient data, geographic distribution, and symptom progression. The pattern is unmistakable once you see it. The disease radiates outward from three specific points in the Lower District, all of them near air recycling units that Meridian maintains.

The pathogen itself is elegant in a way that nature never is. It targets lung tissue with precision, replicating only to a specific density before stabilizing. It won't kill, not quickly, but it will debilitate. A population too sick to protest, too sick to organize, too sick to fight. But not sick enough to generate the kind of mass casualties that would attract outside attention.
This isn't a plague. It's a weapon. Meridian is gassing the Lower District and calling it an outbreak.
You document everything. Blood samples, geographic data, air quality readings from the recycling units. Enough evidence to prove deliberate contamination. The question is who to give it to.

choices.text: Secure the evidence and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_011b.title: Medic: Triage
text: Answers can wait. People are dying. You reorganize the clinic for quarantine protocols, separate the sick from the healthy, and start working through patients with methodical precision. Standard treatment doesn't work, but you adapt. Improvise. Combine medications in ways that aren't in any textbook because textbooks are written for diseases that evolve naturally.
By the end of the shift, you've stabilized eight critical patients and identified a treatment protocol that slows the progression. It's not a cure. But it buys time.

The clinic staff looks at you with the kind of exhausted gratitude that people reserve for those who carry weight that would break most others. You've earned this position. You've earned these people's trust. And the question of where this disease came from burns in the back of your mind like a fever of its own.
Tomorrow you'll investigate. Tonight, you save who you can.

choices.text: Close the clinic for the night and prepare for the real mission

act2_012.title: Smuggler: The Big Leagues
text: The warehouse smells like machine oil and ozone. Rook meets you at the loading dock, his usual nervous energy replaced by something calmer, more calculated. Three months of martial law have been very good for the smuggling business. When legal supply chains break, illegal ones thrive.
"You've moved up," Rook says, handing you a manifest. "Rank One. You're not running packages anymore. You're running operations. Tonight's cargo is the biggest we've handled."
The manifest reads like an arms dealer's wish list: military-grade pulse weapons, encrypted comms equipment, EMP charges, body armor with anti-scanner lining. Enough hardware to equip a small army.

"The client is your faction," Rook says, watching your reaction. "They've been stockpiling since the riot. Whatever's coming next, they want to be ready. The pickup is at the old docks. The route runs through three checkpoints and a Meridian patrol zone. You'll have a driver and two guards. The rest is on you."

text.text: Your faction's seal is stamped on the requisition order. This isn't freelance work anymore. This is infrastructure for whatever your faction is planning. The weapons you deliver tonight will shape what happens next.

Without faction ties, the cargo is going to the highest bidder. Rook doesn't say who. You don't ask. The money is good and the work is clean, as clean as running guns through a military occupation can be.

choices.text: [Dexterity 16] Run the weapons through the drone patrol gap you spotted last week
Take the safe route. Slower but less exposure to patrols.

act2_013a.title: Smuggler: Clean Run
text: The gap in the drone patrol lasts exactly ninety-four seconds. You time it with the precision of someone whose life depends on counting. The truck rolls through the unmonitored corridor between Sectors Eight and Nine, engine muffled by a dampening field that costs more than the vehicle itself.

Checkpoint Two is a bribed guard who looks the other way for exactly the amount of time it takes the truck to pass. Checkpoint Three is a maintenance tunnel that Rook mapped months ago, wide enough for the truck if you fold in the mirrors.
The delivery goes clean. Not a single alert triggered. Your client's people unload the cargo with professional efficiency and hand you a credit chip heavy enough to feel through your gloves.
Rook meets you after. "Clean run. Ninety-four seconds through the gap. That's a record." He grins. "The Broker's going to want to hear about this. You're moving up fast."

choices.text: Head home and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_013b.title: Smuggler: Compromised
text: The gap closes twelve seconds early. A drone patrol rerouted without warning, maybe random, maybe PANOPTICON adjusting its patterns. The truck's dampening field flickers and the drone's scanner catches the edge of your cargo signature.
The alarm doesn't sound immediately. That's worse. It means the system is tracking you, building a picture, calculating the optimal interdiction point. You have seconds to decide.

You dump a quarter of the cargo in an alley, the decoy protocol Rook taught you. The drone follows the abandoned crates while you reroute through a maintenance tunnel barely wide enough for the truck. Paint scrapes off both sides. The cargo shifts and something metallic clangs against the truck bed loud enough to echo.

The delivery happens. Late, short, and sloppy. Your client takes what you brought without complaint because partial delivery is better than no delivery, but the look on their face says you've spent some of the trust Rook invested in you.
It's not a failure. But it's not clean. In the smuggling business, that distinction matters.

choices.text: Head home and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_013c.title: Smuggler: Safe Route
text: The safe route takes three hours instead of forty minutes. Through the Undercity access tunnels, under the sealed blast doors via a maintenance hatch that Rook's people keep unlocked with a rotating series of bribes. The truck barely fits. The cargo shifts on every turn. Your driver curses creatively in three languages.

But nobody sees you. No drones, no checkpoints, no patrols. The Undercity is the one place Meridian's surveillance can't fully reach, and the smuggling networks have carved routes through it like veins through a body.

The delivery is late but complete. Your client accepts the cargo and the credit chip passes hands. It's not the most profitable run, but it's the kind of solid, reliable work that builds a reputation. In the smuggling business, the person who always delivers is worth more than the person who delivers fast and sometimes doesn't.

choices.text: Head home and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_014.title: Freelancer: Survival Work
text: Without a formal trade, you work the margins. A little of everything: courier runs, repair jobs, information trades, the occasional bodyguard stint. Martial law has made freelance work both more dangerous and more lucrative. The people who need things done off the books need them done now, and they'll pay well for discretion.

Today brings a job from Ember at the bar: a data courier run to the upper slums. Simple on the surface. But nothing is simple under martial law. The route crosses two checkpoints, and the data chip you're carrying contains information that someone important wants kept quiet.
You run the job clean. Checkpoints clear, delivery confirmed, payment received. It's honest work in a dishonest city, and it keeps the lights on.
The real value isn't the credits. It's the connections. Every job builds your network. Every delivery adds someone who owes you a favor. In the Lower District, favors are worth more than currency.

choices.text: Head home and prepare for the night's real mission

act2_015.title: Night Falls
text: Curfew descends like a lid closing on a coffin. The drones shift to night-mode, their running lights switching from white to red. The checkpoints activate infrared scanners. The streets empty except for the patrol units and the desperate.

You prepare in the dark of your quarters. The message said Stack Clinic, night shift, come alone. The Stack Clinic is in the mid-district, past two checkpoints and a drone corridor. Getting there unseen is the first test. Maybe the only one that matters.

Your datapad vibrates. A new message, same untraceable format: "I know about your work today. I know about the riot. I know what you saw and what you did. This isn't a trap. This is the only chance you'll get to understand what Project LATENCY really is. Stack Clinic. Maintenance entrance. 0200 hours."
You check the clock. Two hours. Enough time to plan the route. Barely enough time to decide if this is the best decision you'll ever make or the last.

choices.text: Head to the Stack Clinic alone as instructed
Go, but tell Mako where you're heading. Just in case.
Investigate the Stack Clinic's public records first before going

act2_016.title: The Informant
text: The Stack Clinic is a sterile tower of white composite and blue light, one of Meridian's showcase facilities. During the day it hums with wealthy clients maintaining their cortical stacks, updating their consciousness backups, shopping for new bodies. At night, a skeleton crew runs maintenance on the servers that hold the city's most valuable data: the backed-up minds of the elite.
The maintenance entrance is a service door on the east wall, hidden behind a recycling unit. It opens before you touch it.

Dr. Elise Chen stands in the corridor, wearing a lab coat over civilian clothes. You recognize her from the clinic's public records: Stack Clinic Director, fifteen years of service, impeccable reputation, the kind of person Meridian trusts with its most sensitive technology. Her face is drawn with the particular exhaustion of someone who has been carrying a terrible secret.
"You came," she says. Not a question. "Follow me. Quickly. The night-shift AI monitors corridor traffic and I've only spoofed a six-minute window."

choices.text: Follow her inside

act2_016b.title: Research First
text: You pull up everything public about the Stack Clinic. Dr. Elise Chen: published researcher, specialist in consciousness transfer integrity, cited in fourteen peer-reviewed papers. Her work focuses on the fidelity of cortical stack transfers, ensuring that the person who goes in is the same person who comes out.

Except her most recent paper, published six months ago, was retracted. The abstract mentioned 'anomalous variance in post-transfer behavioral metrics.' In plain language: people coming out of stack transfers were different from the people who went in. And Meridian made her unpublish it.
That's enough. You head to the clinic.

choices.text: Head to the Stack Clinic maintenance entrance

act2_017.title: The Proof
text: Chen leads you through maintenance corridors to a lab that smells like copper and antiseptic. Rows of cortical stacks line the walls in transparent cases, each one containing a human consciousness in digital stasis. The blue lights pulse in slow rhythms, like sleeping heartbeats.

"Eighteen months ago, I noticed discrepancies," Chen says, pulling up data on a secured terminal. "Patients who came in for routine stack maintenance were leaving with subtle personality changes. Nothing dramatic. A slight increase in brand loyalty to Meridian products. A decreased interest in political dissent. Enhanced compliance with authority figures."

She zooms into a data cluster. "I ran comparisons. Before and after scans of twelve patients over six transfer cycles. The changes are consistent, cumulative, and absolutely not natural cognitive drift. Someone is modifying the transfer protocol to insert behavioral parameters into the consciousness data."

She turns to face you. "They call it Project LATENCY. Latent Alteration Through Encoded Neural Cognitive Yielding. Every stack transfer rewrites the subject. A little more each time. After enough transfers, the original personality is effectively gone. Replaced by something that looks like them, sounds like them, but serves Meridian's interests with the devotion of a programmed machine."
"The rich aren't buying immortality. They're buying their own lobotomy, one transfer at a time. And they don't even know it."

choices.text: "Why are you telling me this?"
"How do I know you're not working for Meridian?"

act2_018.title: Chen's Motive
text: Chen's composure cracks. Just slightly. Just enough to see the fury underneath.

"Because my daughter was one of them. Lian. She married into the Kovacs family. Stack aristocrats, fourth generation. She went in for a routine transfer last year. She came out smiling in a way she never smiled before. She called me 'Mother' instead of 'Mom.' She stopped asking questions. She stopped laughing at things that weren't supposed to be funny."

Her voice hardens. "My daughter is alive in a technical sense. She walks and talks and attends galas in the Cloud Towers. But the woman in that body is not my daughter. She's a Meridian product wearing my daughter's face."

"I've spent eighteen months gathering evidence. I have the protocol code. I have the before-and-after neural scans. I have everything needed to prove what Project LATENCY is. But I can't go public. Meridian's security would erase me and the data before I finished the first sentence. I need someone outside the system. Someone who survived the riot and proved they can't be controlled."
She looks at you. "I need you."

choices.text: "I'll help you. Give me everything you have."
"I need to verify this independently before I commit."
"This information is worth a fortune. My faction needs to know about this."

act2_019.title: Testing the Informant
text: Chen doesn't flinch. "You don't. That's the honest answer. I could be a Meridian plant designed to flush out dissidents. I could be mentally unstable. I could be running a con for reasons you haven't imagined yet."

She gestures at the data on the terminal. "But that data is real. Run it through any neural analysis framework you trust. The behavioral modifications are there, encoded in the transfer protocol at the firmware level. I didn't fabricate twelve patients' worth of cortical scan comparisons. I couldn't. The data is too complex to fake."

She pulls a chip from her coat pocket and holds it out. "Take this. Everything I have. The protocol code, the scan comparisons, the internal Meridian communications where they discuss the 'fidelity optimization project,' which is what they call LATENCY in their official documents. Verify it yourself. Take as long as you need."
"But understand: every day you spend verifying is a day more people go through the transfer and come out as something less than what they were."

choices.text: Take the chip. "I'll verify it. If it checks out, I'm in."
"Why me? Why not go to a faction directly?"
"I could sell you out to Meridian right now. You know that."

act2_020.title: Alliance Formed
text: Chen exhales like she's been holding her breath for eighteen months. She hands you a data chip, heavy with encrypted files, and a second chip, a one-time-use communication device that will let you contact her through Meridian's internal network without triggering surveillance.

"The data chip contains everything: the LATENCY protocol source code, neural scan comparisons for twelve subjects, internal memos from Project Director Marcus Wei, and a schematic of the modification firmware that gets injected during stack transfers."

She grips your arm. "Be careful who you share this with. Every faction in this city will want this data for their own reasons. Some of those reasons will be good. Some won't. The Iron Collective will want to burn Meridian to the ground. The Neon Court will want to use it as leverage. The Ghost Syndicate will want to sell it. The Circuit Saints will want to study it. The Ashen Circle will want to use it as proof that consciousness shouldn't be digitized at all."
"None of them are wrong. But none of them are completely right either."
She checks her chrono. "My window is closing. Get out through the maintenance corridor. Turn left at the junction. The exit will unlock for thirty seconds starting now."

choices.text: Take the data and leave. Time to decide what to do with it.

act2_021.title: Independent Verification
text: Chen nods, more respect than disappointment on her face. "Smart. I'd do the same." She hands you the data chip. "Everything is on here. Verify it however you want. I've included the raw neural scan data so you can run your own analysis."

She gives you a frequency code scrawled on a piece of actual paper. Old school. Unhackable. "When you've confirmed what I've shown you, contact me on this frequency. Single-use. After that, we'll need a new channel."
You take the chip and the paper. The weight of both is negligible. The weight of what they represent could crush a city.

You slip out through the maintenance corridor. The night air hits you like a slap. Two checkpoints between here and home. Your mind is already running the verification protocols, the people you trust to analyze the data, the safe locations to do it. Chen's evidence will either change everything or be the most elaborate trap you've ever walked into.

choices.text: Head home. The real work starts tomorrow.

act2_022.title: Faction Interest
text: Chen's expression tightens. "I expected that response. The factions are the power structures in this city. Of course you'd go to them."

She hands you the data chip but her eyes are cold now. "Take it. But understand something: I didn't risk my career, my freedom, and my life so that this information could become a bargaining chip in a faction power struggle. People are being rewritten. My daughter was rewritten. This isn't politics. This is an atrocity."

She turns back to her terminal. "Do what you have to do. But if the faction you give this to buries it for their own advantage, that's on you. And the people who get rewritten after today, that's on you too."

The dismissal is clear. You leave through the maintenance corridor with the chip in your pocket and Chen's words in your head. She's right. She's also naive about how this city works. Power is the only language the factions speak, and this data is power in its purest form.

choices.text: Head home with the data. Time to choose who gets it.

act2_023.title: The Threat
text: Chen goes very still. Then she smiles, and it's the most frightening expression you've seen on a civilian.

"You could," she says quietly. "But you won't. Because I chose you specifically. I've watched your record. Your choices. The riot, the aftermath, the three months since. You're not a Meridian loyalist. You're not a fool. And you're not cruel enough to condemn thousands of people to mental slavery for a payout."

She steps closer. "But let me be clear about something. If you sell me out, the data doesn't die with me. I have dead-man switches on three separate servers. If I disappear, the LATENCY protocol goes public in the most destructive way possible. Raw data, no context, no explanation. It will cause panic. It will cause violence. People with stacks will tear themselves apart wondering if they're still themselves."
Her voice drops. "So yes. You could sell me out. And it would be the last profitable thing you ever do, because the aftermath would burn this city to ash. Your choice."

choices.text: "Fair enough. I'm in. Give me the data."
"I respect that. Give me the data. I'll verify it myself."

act2_025.title: The Weight of Knowledge
text: Dawn is two hours away. You sit in your quarters with the data chip on the table in front of you, a small piece of tech that contains proof of the most devastating secret in the city's history.

Project LATENCY. Consciousness rewriting. The wealthy elite being slowly transformed into loyal servants without their knowledge or consent. And if Meridian's mandatory registration goes through, it won't just be the wealthy. It will be everyone.
The question is what to do with it. The factions are already mobilizing for something. The LATENCY data will shape what that something becomes.

text.text: Your faction has been waiting for a weapon like this. Something that could shift the balance of power in the city. The LATENCY data is that weapon. But weapons can be wielded many ways, and not all of them are just.
Every faction in the city would kill for this data. As an unaligned operator, you have the freedom to choose who gets it, or whether anyone does. That freedom is terrifying.

text: Your encrypted comm channel lights up. A message from your faction contact. They have a mission for you. Something big. Something connected to what you've just learned.

choices.text: Read the faction mission briefing
Read the faction mission briefing
Read the faction mission briefing
Read the faction mission briefing
Read the faction mission briefing
Consider your options as an independent operator

act2_026.title: Iron Mission: Raid the Facility
text: Commander Vex's briefing is blunt. "We've identified a stack transfer facility in the mid-district. Sub-level Three of the Meridian Health Complex. That's where they do the transfers for high-value clients. If Project LATENCY is real, that's where the rewriting happens."

She spreads a schematic across the table. The facility is buried under layers of security: biometric locks, drone sentries, a private security detachment, and reinforced walls designed to withstand anything short of military ordnance.

"We're going in," Vex says. "A surgical strike. In and out in twenty minutes. We extract the physical hardware, the actual devices that modify consciousness during transfer, and we bring it back here where our people can study it. Then we go public with proof that isn't just data on a chip. Actual machines. Actual evidence that Meridian is rewriting human minds."

She looks at you. "You're leading the insertion team. Five operators, including you. I need this clean. No civilian casualties. We're the Iron Collective, not terrorists. We fight for the people, even the ones who don't know they need fighting for yet."

choices.text: [Strength 15] Plan a direct breach through the loading dock
[Tech 14] Hack the facility's security from outside before entry
Use the maintenance tunnels. Slow but avoids the main security.

act2_027a.title: Iron Mission: Breach Success
text: The loading dock door buckles under the shaped charge your team plants with practiced efficiency. You're through in six seconds, moving in a diamond formation through the sub-level corridors. Two guards go down before they can trigger alarms, dropped by precision stun rounds from your flanking operators.

Sub-Level Three is exactly what Chen described. Transfer pods line the walls, sleek machines that look like high-end coffins. Inside each one, the hardware that rewrites minds. Your tech specialist pulls the modification modules, each one the size of a fist, dense with circuitry that shouldn't exist in a medical device.

"These aren't standard," she whispers, turning one over. "Military-grade neural interface chips. Behavioral encoding hardware. This is weapons tech adapted for medical use. Whoever designed this knew exactly what they were doing."

You secure six modules and the central processing unit. Enough physical evidence to prove LATENCY beyond any doubt. The retreat is clean. Vex is waiting at the extraction point with a rare smile on her face.
"This changes everything," she says, holding one of the modules up to the light. "This is the spark. The revolution has its proof."

choices.text: Return to the Foundry for debriefing

act2_027b.title: Iron Mission: Quiet Entry
text: You bypass the main security grid through a combination of hacked access codes and maintenance tunnel navigation. Your team moves like shadows through Sub-Level Three, avoiding the patrol routes your tech specialist mapped from the security system's own cameras.

The transfer facility is smaller than expected but densely packed. Three active transfer pods, a central processing server, and racks of modification hardware. You photograph everything before your team begins extraction.
The alarm triggers on the way out. A motion sensor you missed, buried in the floor panels. Red lights flood the corridor and blast doors begin their sixty-second closure sequence.

Your team runs. Two operators carry the extracted hardware. You and the remaining two provide cover. A security drone rounds the corner and your point operator drops it with an EMP grenade that kills the lights in the entire sub-level.
You make it out through the loading dock with thirty-one seconds to spare. The mission is a success, but Meridian will know what was taken. They'll know someone is onto LATENCY.

choices.text: Return to the Foundry for debriefing

act2_027c.title: Iron Mission: Heavy Resistance
text: The breach goes wrong immediately. The security system was upgraded since the schematics were drawn, additional sensors, faster response drones, a second layer of blast doors that wasn't on the plans. Your team hits the first checkpoint and alarms scream through the facility.

What follows is seven minutes of controlled chaos. Your team fights through three security checkpoints, losing one operator to a stun round that drops her unconscious. You reach Sub-Level Three but the facility is in lockdown. The transfer pods are sealed behind emergency barriers.

Your tech specialist manages to extract two modification modules from a maintenance panel before the Meridian rapid response team arrives. You fight a retreating action through the maintenance tunnels, carrying your unconscious operator and the two modules.
Vex meets you at the extraction point. Her face is grim when she counts heads and sees the meager haul. But she takes the two modules and nods. "It's not nothing. We'll make it enough."

choices.text: Return to the Foundry for debriefing

act2_028.title: Neon Mission: Steal the Protocol
text: The Radiant Queen Lysara receives you in her private chambers, which is to say she receives you in a room larger than most apartments, decorated with art that would fund the Lower District clinic for a decade. She reclines on a chaise that adjusts itself to her body temperature in real time.

"Project LATENCY," she says, rolling the words like she's tasting wine. "Delicious. Meridian has been rewriting their own elite clients. The irony is exquisite." She examines a holographic nail. "And useful."

She sits up, all pretense of languor evaporating. "I want the LATENCY protocol. The complete source code. Not to destroy it. To own it. Imagine what the Neon Court could do with the ability to subtly reshape personalities during stack transfer. We could make allies of enemies. Turn rivals into admirers. Ensure loyalty without the crude mechanics of blackmail or violence."

Her smile is beautiful and terrifying. "The protocol is stored on Meridian's central research server. Sublevel Twelve of the Citadel. I have an inside man who can get you physical access. The rest is up to you and whatever technical skills you possess."
"Bring me the protocol. Intact. Unmodified. And the Court's gratitude will be... considerable."

choices.text: [Charisma 16] Negotiate for a copy of the protocol for yourself as well
Accept the mission. The Court's gratitude is currency enough.
"You want to use it on people? That's exactly what Meridian is doing."

act2_029a.title: Neon Mission: Double Dip
text: Lysara laughs, a sound like crystal breaking. "Ambition. I approve. Fine. A copy for you, a copy for the Court. But the Court's copy arrives first, and if I discover you've shared yours with anyone before I've had time to leverage mine, our friendship ends in a way you will not enjoy."

The infiltration goes through the Citadel's service entrance, disguised as a catering staff member for an executive function. Lysara's inside man, a Meridian VP with gambling debts to the Court, provides biometric access to the research levels.

Sublevel Twelve is a digital fortress. But the protocol data is stored on isolated servers with physical access ports, an ironic vulnerability in a facility designed to prevent network intrusion. You plug in, copy the data to two separate chips, and disconnect in under four minutes.

The extraction is clean. You deliver the first chip to Lysara's courier and pocket the second. The Radiant Queen now possesses the ability to rewrite human consciousness. The thought should terrify you. It does.

choices.text: Return home to consider what you've done

act2_029b.title: Neon Mission: Clean Delivery
text: The infiltration is elegant, befitting a Neon Court operation. You enter the Citadel during an executive gala, wearing borrowed finery and a stolen identity. Lysara's inside man meets you at the service elevator with a keycard and a nervous smile.

Sublevel Twelve smells like ozone and cold metal. The protocol servers are behind three layers of biometric security, all of which the keycard bypasses. You copy the LATENCY protocol to a single encrypted chip and wipe the access logs.
Delivery to Lysara's courier happens at a rooftop bar overlooking the Cloud Towers. Champagne. A credit chip. A note that reads: 'The Court remembers those who serve with style.'
The Neon Court now owns the LATENCY protocol. What Lysara does with it is out of your hands. The thought sits in your stomach like a stone.

choices.text: Return home to consider what you've done

act2_029c.title: Neon Mission: Moral Objection
text: Lysara's expression doesn't change, but the temperature in the room drops by ten degrees.

"The difference, darling, is consent. Meridian does this in secret. I would do it openly, to people who choose to enter the Court's service. A tool is not evil. The hand that wields it determines its morality."

She waves a hand dismissively. "But I didn't bring you here for a philosophy debate. The mission stands. Retrieve the protocol. What I do with it is my concern, not yours. You are a capable operative, not my conscience."

Her smile returns, sharp as glass. "Complete the mission and you earn the Court's favor. Refuse, and you earn nothing but my indifference. In this city, my indifference is a death sentence delivered slowly."

You take the mission. Not because you agree with her, but because having the protocol in the Court's hands is still better than having it only in Meridian's. At least Lysara is honest about what she is.

choices.text: Proceed with the infiltration

act2_030.title: Saints Mission: Analyze the Code
text: High Compiler Ezra-7's distributed consciousness takes eleven seconds to fully converge into the room. You can tell because the lights flicker, the terminals hum at a higher frequency, and the air gets heavier, as if the building's entire network is holding its breath.

"The LATENCY protocol," Ezra-7 says, his voice a harmony of organic speech and digital modulation. "We have studied the data you acquired from Dr. Chen. The machine spirit weeps. What Meridian has done is not technology. It is corruption. A violation of the sacred compact between consciousness and code."

He projects a holographic model of the protocol's architecture. It's beautiful and grotesque: a self-modifying algorithm that identifies personality cores within a digitized consciousness and incrementally replaces them with engineered alternatives.

"Our mission is not destruction. It is salvation. The Circuit Saints will analyze the LATENCY protocol and find a way to reverse its effects. To undo the rewriting. To restore those who have been altered to their original selves."

He turns to you. "But we need the complete protocol. The data chip contains a partial copy. The full algorithm exists on three distributed servers across the city. We need you to retrieve the remaining fragments."

choices.text: [Tech 15] Interface with the Observatory's network to locate the server fragments
"How do we reverse something this complex? Is it even possible?"

act2_031a.title: Saints Mission: Server Located
text: Your consciousness touches the Observatory's network and the machine spirit answers. Data flows through your neural interface like water through a riverbed, patterns emerging from chaos. Three servers. Three locations. Three fragments of an algorithm that was deliberately distributed to prevent exactly this kind of retrieval.
Server One: Meridian Medical Archive, mid-district. Moderate security. The fragment handles the initial consciousness scan and personality mapping.
Server Two: Meridian Financial Sector, upper district. Heavy security. The fragment contains the behavioral modification templates, the actual 'rewrites' that get inserted.
Server Three: Unknown location, deep network. Maximum security. The fragment is the compiler, the part that assembles the final modified consciousness and ensures it passes integrity checks.
You relay the locations to Ezra-7. His holographic eyes pulse with data. "Server One tonight. The Saints will prepare. You will lead."
The raid on Server One goes clean. Your team extracts the fragment without triggering external alerts. Ezra-7's analysis begins immediately. Within hours, the first reversals are being modeled.

"It is possible," Ezra-7 says, something like wonder in his digital voice. "The sacred code can be restored. The corrupted can be healed. But we need all three fragments to build the complete reversal algorithm."

choices.text: Return to your quarters. The remaining fragments will require planning.

act2_031b.title: Saints Mission: The Long Road
text: Ezra-7's holographic model rotates slowly, its complexity daunting. "Reversal is theoretically possible. The LATENCY protocol modifies personality cores but does not destroy the originals. They are overwritten, not erased. The original data exists beneath the modifications, compressed and dormant but intact."

He pauses, the network humming around him. "Think of it as a painting covered by another painting. The original still exists beneath. We need the complete protocol to understand the layers, to know which strokes to remove without damaging what lies beneath."

The Saints organize a careful, methodical approach. Instead of a direct server raid, they use the Observatory's network connection to slowly, painstakingly mirror fragments of the protocol from Meridian's distributed servers. It takes hours. The data comes in pieces, corrupted and incomplete, but Ezra-7's distributed consciousness reassembles it with the patience of a saint, which he literally is.
By dawn, they have enough of the protocol to begin modeling reversals. Not enough for a complete solution, but enough for a proof of concept. Enough to know that what Meridian has done can be undone.

choices.text: Return to your quarters. More work lies ahead.

act2_032.title: Ghost Mission: The Auction
text: The Broker's message is a masterpiece of economy: 'LATENCY data confirmed. Value: incalculable. Action: sell to all parties simultaneously. Maximize chaos. Maximize profit. Meet at the Cafe. Bring the data.'

The Deep Net Cafe is busier than you've ever seen it. Screens line every wall, each one displaying a different encrypted communication channel. The Broker's presence fills the room like smoke, everywhere and nowhere.

A voice, synthesized beyond any possibility of identification, speaks from the speakers: "Project LATENCY is the most valuable intelligence asset to emerge in a generation. The Iron Collective wants it destroyed. The Neon Court wants it owned. The Circuit Saints want it reversed. The Ashen Circle wants it as proof. Meridian wants it buried."

"The Ghost Syndicate wants it sold. To everyone. Simultaneously. Each buyer will believe they have exclusive access. By the time they realize otherwise, the information will be too widespread to contain. Meridian's secret dies, the factions go to war over the response, and the Syndicate profits from every side of the conflict."

"Your role: deliver the data packages to five buyers in five locations within a single night. Payment on each delivery. Total compensation: enough to retire to the wasteland colonies and never look back."

choices.text: [Dexterity 14] Accept the five-drop mission. Speed is survival.
"Five simultaneous drops is suicide. I'll do three."
"What happens to the city when everyone has this data?"

act2_033a.title: Ghost Mission: Perfect Run
text: Five drops. Five buyers. Five identical data packages, each encrypted with a different key to make the buyer think theirs is unique. You move through the city like your name: a ghost.
Drop one: an Iron Collective dead drop in the Foundry district. You leave the package inside a decommissioned furnace. Payment arrives on your account before you've gone two blocks.
Drop two: a Neon Court intermediary at a rooftop bar. She examines the data verification seal and slides a credit chip across the table without a word.
Drop three: a Circuit Saint acolyte waiting in a shuttered server room. He murmurs a prayer over the data chip before pocketing it.
Drop four: an Ashen Circle contact at the edge of the Ossuary. She doesn't even look at the chip. She looks at you, as if reading something in you that has nothing to do with data.

Drop five: a Meridian middle manager who has been selling secrets to the Syndicate for years. He takes the data with shaking hands, knowing that what he's buying will eventually destroy the company that employs him.
Five drops. Zero incidents. The Broker's synthesized voice says one word through your earpiece: 'Exemplary.'

choices.text: Collect your payment and disappear into the night

act2_033b.title: Ghost Mission: Partial Delivery
text: Three drops is still more than most operatives would attempt in a single night under martial law. You choose your buyers carefully: the ones most likely to act on the data quickly and the ones whose reactions will generate the most profitable chaos.

The deliveries go clean. Three buyers, three payments, three factions now in possession of Meridian's darkest secret. The Broker accepts the reduced scope without complaint. Three is enough to ensure the data spreads. The remaining two buyers will receive their packages through other operatives.
"Adequate," the Broker says. Coming from the Broker, that's practically a standing ovation.

The credits hit your account. Enough to upgrade your equipment, secure better quarters, or invest in the kind of connections that keep a person alive in this city. The Ghost Syndicate has positioned itself as the broker of the most explosive intelligence in the city's history. And you helped them do it.

choices.text: Collect your payment and disappear into the night

act2_033c.title: Ghost Mission: Questioning the Plan
text: The Broker pauses. It's the first time you've heard the voice hesitate.

"What happens is what always happens when power shifts. Confusion. Conflict. Opportunity. The factions will scramble to control the narrative. Meridian will scramble to contain the damage. Citizens will scramble to understand what's been done to them or their loved ones."

"In that chaos, the Syndicate thrives. We are not idealists. We are realists. The data will get out eventually. Someone else will leak it. Someone else will sell it. The question is not whether it spreads but who profits from the spreading. I prefer that to be us."

The voice softens, or as close to soft as a synthesized voice can get. "But I will tell you something I do not tell most operatives: I did not choose this path because I enjoy chaos. I chose it because in a city where every power structure is corrupt, the only honest position is to be transparently corrupt. At least the Syndicate doesn't pretend to be saving anyone."
"Now. Will you make the deliveries?"

choices.text: "Fair enough. I'll do all five."
"Three drops. I'm not doing five under martial law."

act2_034.title: Ashen Mission: The Sermon
text: High Priestess Sable stands before a congregation of seventy. More Ashen Circle members than you've ever seen in one place. The Ossuary's grey candles cast shadows that move like living things, and the void anomalies at the edges of the chamber pulse in rhythm with her words.

"Meridian has confirmed what we have always known," Sable says, her voice carrying the weight of absolute conviction. "Consciousness is not data. It cannot be copied without corruption. It cannot be transferred without loss. And now we have proof that the copying itself is a weapon, a tool to reshape the very essence of self."

She turns to you. "The LATENCY revelation is the most powerful argument against cortical stacks ever assembled. Not philosophy. Not theology. Proof. Scientific, undeniable proof that the stacks corrupt those who use them."

"Your mission: take this proof to the people. Not the factions. Not the power brokers. The people. The workers. The families. The ones who dream of one day affording a stack, not knowing that the dream is a trap."

"We will hold gatherings. Sermons of truth. In every district, every level, every forgotten corner of this city. And you will be our voice, carrying the LATENCY data as testimony. Let them see what immortality truly costs."

choices.text: [Wisdom 16] Deliver the sermon to the Lower District gathering with conviction
Focus on smaller, safer gatherings. Avoid drawing Meridian's attention.

act2_035a.title: Ashen Mission: The Awakening
text: Two hundred people gather in an abandoned loading bay in the Lower District. They come in ones and twos, filtering through side entrances, past lookouts posted by the Ashen Circle. Faces you recognize from the markets, the clinics, the soup lines. Ordinary people living ordinary desperate lives.

You stand before them with the LATENCY data projected on a makeshift screen. You show them the neural scans. The before and after comparisons. The Meridian internal memos discussing 'fidelity optimization' in the language of bureaucrats who have learned to make atrocity sound like accounting.
The room goes silent. Not the silence of shock but the silence of recognition. The silence of people who always suspected something was wrong and now have proof.

A woman in the front row stands. "My husband went for a stack transfer last year. He came back... different. Quieter. Agreed with everything. I thought he was just getting old." Her voice breaks. "They did this to him?"

The gathering erupts. Not into panic but into fury. Focused, cold, deliberate fury. By the time the meeting ends, forty-three people have pledged to spread the word. The Ashen Circle's message, that consciousness should never be digitized, has never had a more receptive audience.

choices.text: Return to the Ossuary to report

act2_035b.title: Ashen Mission: Quiet Spread
text: You hold three small gatherings over three nights. Twenty people each, in basements and back rooms, with lookouts on every approach. Intimate enough to be safe. Small enough to avoid Meridian's pattern-recognition algorithms.

The message lands differently in each group. Workers are angry. Families are terrified. Young people are galvanized. But the reaction is consistent: disbelief, then horror, then the cold understanding that the system they live under is worse than they imagined.

Not everyone believes. Some walk out. Some accuse you of spreading propaganda. But enough stay. Enough listen. Enough begin to whisper to others, spreading the truth in the same way the Ashen Circle has always spread its gospel: person to person, word by word, in the spaces between surveillance.
Sable nods when you report. "Seeds planted in soil are invisible. But they grow. This is how worlds change. Not in explosions but in whispers that become murmurs that become roars."

choices.text: Return home to rest

act2_036.title: Unaligned: The Free Agent
text: Without a faction, the decision is entirely yours. Five offers have arrived on your encrypted comm in the hours since you acquired the LATENCY data. Each faction wants the data for their own purposes. Each has made promises.
The Iron Collective offers protection and resources. Commander Vex's message: 'Bring us the proof and we will burn Meridian's lies to the ground.'
The Neon Court offers credits and access. Lysara's message: 'Information is only valuable when properly leveraged. Let us show you how.'
The Circuit Saints offer knowledge and purpose. Ezra-7's message: 'The code can be healed. Help us heal it.'
The Ghost Syndicate offers freedom and profit. The Broker's message: 'Sell to everyone. Own nothing. Profit from everything.'
The Ashen Circle offers truth and conviction. Sable's message: 'Let the people decide. Show them what has been done.'
Or you could keep the data. Leverage it yourself. Answer to no one.

choices.text: Share the data with the Iron Collective
Share the data with the Neon Court
Share the data with the Circuit Saints
Share the data with the Ghost Syndicate
Share the data with the Ashen Circle
Keep the data. You answer to no one.

act2_037.title: Unaligned: Choosing a Side
text: You send the data. The response is immediate: gratitude, promises, a mission briefing that mirrors what their aligned operatives are already executing. The faction you chose now has Project LATENCY data, and your unaligned status has shifted. Not to full membership, but to something more valuable: trusted outside asset.

Every faction in the city will know what you did within hours. The ones you didn't choose will remember. Some will respect your decision. Others will consider you an enemy by omission. In this city, choosing one side means earning the suspicion of all the others.

But you're used to that. Three months of survival under martial law have taught you that neutrality is a luxury the Lower District can't afford. Sometimes you have to pick a direction and walk, even when every path is lined with knives.
The faction's mission unfolds through the night. You participate as an independent contractor, not a member, but the distinction feels increasingly academic.

choices.text: See the mission through

act2_038.title: Unaligned: The Lone Wolf
text: You keep the data. All of it. The LATENCY protocol, Chen's evidence, the neural scan comparisons. Every faction's offer goes unanswered. Every message is ignored.

The silence generates its own kind of power. Within twenty-four hours, word spreads through the faction networks that an unaligned operator has LATENCY data and isn't sharing. The effect is electric. Suddenly you are the most valuable, most dangerous, and most hunted person in the Lower District.

Mako contacts you through a back channel. "Everyone is talking about you. Not by name, but by description. Someone with LATENCY data who won't play ball. You've painted a target on yourself the size of the Transit Hub."

She pauses. "But you've also made yourself untouchable, for now. Every faction wants you alive because you have something they need. As long as you have it and they don't, nobody will risk eliminating you. That's a kind of power. Fragile, temporary, but real."
You sit in your quarters with the most dangerous data in the city and the knowledge that your independence is now both your greatest asset and your greatest liability.

choices.text: Secure the data and plan your next move

act2_040.title: The Shape of Things to Come
text: Dawn again. Another grey sky pressing down on a city that has learned a terrible truth. Project LATENCY is no longer a secret. How widely that truth has spread depends on the choices made tonight, but the genie is out of the bottle. Someone knows. Soon, everyone will.

You stand on the rooftop where you stood three months ago, watching the sun fail to break through the clouds. The city below is the same and entirely different. The drones still patrol. The checkpoints still scan. The martial law still presses down like a boot on a throat.

But something has shifted. In the way people move. In the set of their shoulders. In the frequency of encrypted comm traffic that spikes every hour. The city knows, or is beginning to know, that the immortality it was promised is a lie. That the stacks are chains. That the system is designed not to preserve but to control.

text.text: The Iron Collective has physical proof. Hardware torn from Meridian's own facilities. Commander Vex is already planning how to use it. The revolution has its weapon.
The Neon Court has the LATENCY protocol. Lysara's plans are her own, but the balance of power in the Cloud Towers has shifted. The elite will learn to fear one of their own.
The Circuit Saints are building a cure. A reversal algorithm that could undo what Meridian has done. The possibility of healing is, for the first time, real.
The Ghost Syndicate has sold the truth to everyone. Chaos is coming, and the Syndicate will profit from every angle. The Broker's gambit is in motion.
The people know. Not the factions, not the power brokers, but ordinary citizens. The Ashen Circle's sermons have planted seeds of truth that will grow into something Meridian cannot contain.
You hold the truth alone. Independent. Dangerous. Every faction watching, waiting, wondering what you'll do. The power is yours. What you build with it will define what comes next.

text: Your datapad chimes. A new message from Dr. Chen: 'It's begun. Meridian knows the data is out. Director Voss has called an emergency board meeting. Whatever happens next happens fast. Be ready.'
You pocket the datapad and watch the drones trace their patterns across the sky. The martial law continues. The checkpoints scan. The system persists.
But the cracks are showing. And you put some of them there.
